content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


May 5, 1993




Honorable Pete McHugh
Mayor, City of Milpitas
455 E. Calaveras Blvd.
Milpitas, CA 95035

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-142

Dear Mayor McHugh:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities as Mayor of Milpitas under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

1. Have you received a "gift" by virtue of a discounted automobile lease program provided by the Ford Motor Company available to you as the Mayor of Milpitas?

2. If you have received a "gift" from the Ford Motor Company, will the gift create a conflict of interest with respect to city council decisions affecting a shopping mall proposed by the Ford Motor Land Company and the development firm of Petrie, Dierman, and Kughn?

_________________________
1. Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-142
Page 2


     3.  If you have received a "gift" from the Ford Motor
Company, will the gift create a conflict of interest with respect
to city council decisions on appointments to the city's planning
commission, which will ultimately make decisions affecting the
shopping mall proposed by the Ford Motor Land Company and the
development firm of Petrie, Dierman, and Kughn?

CONCLUSION

     1.  A discount on an automobile lease from the Ford Motor
Company constitutes a gift.

     2.  Since you have received a gift of more than $250 from the
Ford Motor Company, you may not participate in any decision which
will foreseeably and materially affect the Ford Motor Company or
any business entity which is a parent, subsidiary, or otherwise
related to the Ford Motor Company.  Since the Ford Motor Land
Company is the applicant with respect to the mall project, you may
not participate in the mall project.

     3.  However, so long as you have no financial interest in the
person you wish to appoint to the planning commission (such as
having received income or gifts from the individual), and so long
as there is no agreement as to how the appointee might vote on any
given project, you may participate in the appointment decision.

FACTS

     You are the mayor of the City of Milpitas.  You and your city
participate in the Ford Motor Company's Governor/Mayor Vehicle
Program.  Pursuant to this program, mayors and governors of
jurisdictions where Ford Motor Plants are located may lease a new
Ford automobile from the Ford Motor Company.  The annual cost of
the program is $4,360.  You stated that the City of Milpitas pays
you, and other councilmembers and department heads, a car
allowance of $4,200 as part of your compensation.  You apply this
money and $160 in personal funds to pay the annual lease price of
$4,360.

     However, the lease price that the Ford Motor Company is
charging participants is less than the fair market value of the
lease.  Additionally, at no extra charge, the Ford Motor Company
pays for the automobile insurance.  According to the information
provided by your city attorney, which was submitted with your
request, a comparable lease of the Ford vehicle for nonprogram
participants is approximately $5,400 annually.

     The city council will soon be considering a proposed retail
shopping mall to be built by the Ford Land Motor Company and the
development firm of Petrie, Dierman, and Kughn.  You have become
concerned that by virtue of the discounted lease you may have a
conflict of interest in the decision.

Our File No. A-93-142
Page 3


ANALYSIS

I.  Characterization of the Lease

    A.  Payments from the City

    According to your facts, the city of Milpitas pays you, and
other councilmembers and department heads, a car allowance of
$4,200 as part of your compensation. Reimbursement for expenses
or per diem received from a local governmental agency is excluded
from the definition of income pursuant to Section 82030(b)(2).
(Fogelman Advice Letter, No. I-90-618.) Thus, this portion of the
automobile lease is not reportable nor an economic interest under
the Act.

    B.  Discounts

    However, Section 82028(a) defines a "gift" as:

        [A]ny payment to the extent that consideration
    of equal or greater value is not received and
    includes a rebate or discount in the price of
    anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
    made in the regular course of business to members
    of the public without regard to official status.
    Any person, other than a defendant in a criminal
    action, who claims that a payment is not a gift by
    reason of receipt of consideration has the burden
    of proving that the consideration received is of
    equal or greater value.

                                    (Emphasis added.)

    You stated you use the $4,200 city allowance and $160 in
personal funds to pay the annual lease price of $4,360. However,
according to your facts, the lease price that the Ford Motor
Company is charging you is less than the fair market value of the
lease.  Pursuant to Section 82028, the discount in the price is a
gift unless the discount is made in the regular course of business
to members of the public without regard to official status.

    Moreover, under your facts the donor of the discount would be
the Ford Motor Company. Regulation 18945(a)(1) provides that if a
donor makes a payment to a third party, such as the city, and in
fact directs and controls the use of the payment to make a gift to
one or more clearly identified official, the donor is the source
of the gift to the official.2

________________________

    2  Under some circumstances we have treated payments to a local
government agency as a gift to the agency rather than the official
who receives the benefit.  (See e.g., In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC
Ops. 52, copy enclosed.)  However, this exception would not apply
to your facts since the discount was designated for the mayor.

Our File No. A-93-142
Page 4


Finally, since the car discount is accessible solely to
mayors and governors and the discount in the price is not made in
the regular course of business to members of the public without
regard to official status, the discount constitutes a gift.3

Please note, you stated that free insurance coverage is also
provided without any charge. This would constitute an additional
gift from the Ford Motor Company of the amount that it would have
cost you to obtain similar insurance coverage.

C. Return, Donation, or Reimbursement of Gifts

Under certain circumstances, gifts may be returned,
reimbursed, or donated to charity in order to decline the gift.
Regulation 18726.1 provides:

     (1) The official may return the gift to the
donor or his or her agent or intermediary, unused,
within 30 days of receipt.

     (2) The official may, within 30 days of
receipt, donate the gift, unused, to a charitable
organization without claiming it as a charitable
contribution for tax purposes.

     (3) The official, may within 30 days of
receipt, donate the gift, unused, to a state, local
or federal government agency without claiming any
deduction for tax purposes.

     (4) The official may, within 30 days of
receipt, reimburse the donor or his or her agent or
intermediary for all or a portion of the gift.

Regulation 18726.1(b).

For example, you may reimburse the donor or his or her agent
or intermediary for all or a portion of the gift. Webster's New
World Dictionary (1988) defines the word "reimburse" as "to pay
back (money spent)" or "to repay or compensate (a person) for
expenses, damages, losses, etc." (See also, Nolan Advice Letter,
No. A-84-293.) We have previously interpreted "reimbursement" to
be by cash payment and not by any other means.


3 In the documents that you submitted with your request, your
city attorney estimates that the fair market value of the lease is
$450 per month for two years, or $5,400 annually. If this is an
accurate assessment of the fair market value, the annual gift you
will receive is $1,040, the difference between your payments plus
the city car allowance, and the fair market value.

Our File No. A-93-142
Page 5


II. Public Official's Obligations Regarding Gifts

    A. Conflicts of Interest: Shopping Mall Decisions

Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any level of
state or local government from making, participating in making or
in any way attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distributable
from its effect on the public generally, on any donor of, or any
intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating
$250 or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made. (Section 87103(e).)

Consequently, you may not participate in any decision if it
is reasonably foreseeable* that the decision will have a material
financial effect on a source of gifts of $250 or more.

The test for materiality differs depending on the specific
circumstances of each decision. Where a source of gifts is
directly before the city council, Regulation 18702.1(a) provides
that the effect of the decision on the source of gifts is deemed
to be material and disqualification is required. (Combs Advice
Letter, No. A-89-177.)

A source of gifts is directly before the city council when
the source initiates the proceeding by filing an application,
claim, appeal, or similar request, or is a named party in, or the
subject of, the proceeding. A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or
business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)


4 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Our File No. A-93-142
Page 6


Since the Ford Motor Land Company⁵ is an applicant with
respect to the shopping mall decisions, you may not participate in
those decisions. Where the source of gifts is not directly before
the city council, but may be indirectly affected, Regulation
18702.2 applies.⁶

B. Conflicts of Interest: Appointments

You also asked about your ability to appoint persons to the
planning commission since they will be required, in the future, to
make decisions affecting the mall. For example, in the Benjamin
Advice Letter, No. A-86-148, we advised:

Disqualification is personal as to the
official and does not prohibit the agency from
acting without the official's input and
participation. This scheme obviously envisions
that the disqualified official may be supplanted in
the decision-making process where delegation or
transfer of the decision is appropriate.
[citations omitted] This is the case so long as
the disqualified official does not make,
participate in making, or use his/her official
position to influence the making of the decision by
the person to whom the decision is delegated.

* * *

So long as a disqualified public official does
not seek in any way to influence the decision of a
stand-in as to the specific decision as to which
disqualification is required, no impropriety exists
in turning that decision over to another person to
make.

[The councilmember] is not alleged to have a
financial interest in the appointment of a
replacement on the Redevelopment Agency Board.
Rather, it is contended that he has a disqualifying
financial interest in the decisions to be made by
the Redevelopment Agency Board, which his
replacement would participate in making. Absent a
specific agreement between [the councilmember] and
the appointee that the latter will vote in a
particular manner on a particular decision, it is

⁵ We assume for purposes of this letter that the Ford Motor
Company and the Ford Motor Land Company are affiliated entities.
(See e.g., Regulation 18236, copy enclosed.)

⁶ We have enclosed a copy of Regulation 18702.2 for your
information.

Our File No. A-93-142
Page 7


        not reasonably foreseeable that the appointment of
any given person will affect [the councilmember's]
financial interest.

        Applied to your facts, so long as you have no financial
interest in the person you wish to appoint to the planning
commission (such as having received income or gifts from the
individual), and so long as there is no agreement as to how the
appointee might vote on any given project, you may participate in
the appointment decision.7  (Benjamin Advice Letter, No. A-86-148;
Lofgren Advice Letter, No. A-86-307, copies enclosed.)

C.  Disclosure

        The Act also requires that every public official disclose all
the official's economic interests that could foreseeably be
affected by the exercise of the official's duties. (Sections
81002(c), 87200-87313.) As a city councilmember for the City of
Milpitas, you are required to disclose statutorily specified
investments, real property interests, sources of income and
sources of gifts.

        Section 87207 provides:

        (a)  When income is required to be reported
under this article, the statement shall contain...:

                (1) The name and address of each source
of income aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value, or fifty
dollars ($50) or more in value if the income
was a gift, and a general description of the
business activity, if any, of each source;

                (2) A statement whether the aggregate
value of income from each source, or in the
case of a loan, the highest amount owed to
each source, was at least two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) but did not exceed one thousand
dollars ($1,000), whether it was in excess of
one thousand dollars ($1,000) but was not
greater than ten thousand dollars ($10,000),
or whether it was greater than ten thousand
dollars ($10,000);

                (3) A description of the consideration,
if any, for which the income was received;


_________________________
7  Of course, once a replacement has been appointed, you may in no
way attempt to influence the decisions of the planning commission
as to decisions in which you will have a conflict of interest.

Our File No. A-93-142
Page 8


(4) In the case of a gift, the amount
and the date on which the gift was received.

(5) In the case of a loan, the annual
interest rate and the security, if any, given
for the loan.

D. Gift Limits

Additionally, the Act now provides gift and honoraria limits
with respect to local elected officers. Section 89501 provides:
No local elected officeholder shall accept any honoraria for any
speech given, article published, or attendance at any public or
private conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or
like gathering, or any gifts, from any single source, which is in
excess of $1,000, in any calendar year, except reimbursement for
actual travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in connection
therewith.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.8

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division


8 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

once a replacement has been appointed,
you may in no
way attempt to influence the decisions of the planning commission
as to decisions in which you will have a conflict of interest.


Our File No.
A—93-l42
(4)
In the case of a gift, the amount
and the date on which the gift was received.
(5)
In the case of a loan, the annual
interest rate and the security, if any,
given
for the loan.
D.
Gift Limits
Additionally, the Act now provides gift and honoraria limits
with respect to local elected officers.
Section 89501 provides:
No local elected officeholder shall accept any honoraria for any
speech given, article published,
or attendance at any public or
private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal,
or
like gathering,
or any gifts,
from any single source, which is in
excess of $1,000,
in any calendar year, except reimbursement for
actual travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in connection
therewith.

CONCLUSION: 1.

Company constitutes a gift.
2.

Ford Motor Company,
you may not participate in any decision which

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission May 5, 1993 Honorable Pete McHugh Mayor, City of Milpitas 455 E. Calaveras Blvd. Milpitas, CA 95035 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-93-142 Dear Mayor McHugh: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities as Mayor of Milpitas under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTION 1. Have you received a "gift" by virtue of a discounted automobile lease program provided by the Ford Motor Company available to you as the Mayor of Milpitas? 2. If you have received a "gift" from the Ford Motor Company, will the gift create a conflict of interest with respect to city council decisions affecting a shopping mall proposed by the Ford Motor Land Company and the development firm of Petrie, Dierman, and Kughn? _________________________ 1. Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-93-142 Page 2 3. If you have received a "gift" from the Ford Motor Company, will the gift create a conflict of interest with respect to city council decisions on appointments to the city's planning commission, which will ultimately make decisions affecting the shopping mall proposed by the Ford Motor Land Company and the development firm of Petrie, Dierman, and Kughn? CONCLUSION 1. A discount on an automobile lease from the Ford Motor Company constitutes a gift. 2. Since you have received a gift of more than $250 from the Ford Motor Company, you may not participate in any decision which will foreseeably and materially affect the Ford Motor Company or any business entity which is a parent, subsidiary, or otherwise related to the Ford Motor Company. Since the Ford Motor Land Company is the applicant with respect to the mall project, you may not participate in the mall project. 3. However, so long as you have no financial interest in the person you wish to appoint to the planning commission (such as having received income or gifts from the individual), and so long as there is no agreement as to how the appointee might vote on any given project, you may participate in the appointment decision. FACTS You are the mayor of the City of Milpitas. You and your city participate in the Ford Motor Company's Governor/Mayor Vehicle Program. Pursuant to this program, mayors and governors of jurisdictions where Ford Motor Plants are located may
